next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

Mira kept Gabe's boots in her lap because the triage tech had cut the laces off and tossed them at her like waste. Every time the automatic doors opened, cold salt air came in off the street and lifted the smell the emergency ward still couldn't beat by midnight: wet ash, burned insulation, fryer grease from the all-night stand across the avenue, and under it all the sweet cooked stink of cauterized tissue drifting from somebody else's curtain. The left sole was worn harder at the outer heel, the drag mark he always pretended she imagined.

The baton strike had landed across old damage. That was the sentence the admitting resident used, clean enough to chart. Mira had seen Gabe's back when they rolled him to imaging. The bruise was still coming up, a long dark band crossing the pale ladder of old surgery scars, already swelling around the places his body had never repaired right the first time. The hospital gown would not close over the brace line. He kept one hand behind him anyway, trying to hold the thin fabric together out of reflex, modesty surviving things that dignity did not.

Jo stood at the foot of the bed with her key ring wound twice around one detergent-dried hand. Half the tags were blackened. Her practical shoes left damp gray prints on the floor every time she crossed from bed to window and back. She had spent the last hour calling the fire desk, her landlord, two laundry customers whose winter things had still been in the dryers, and somebody's cousin who knew how long municipal seals tended to stay on burned storefronts when poor people owned...
open_hooks_to_carry: Make the raid materially confusing and bodily costly, not cinematic clean.
Let Jo, Tavi, Gabe, and Lin each make a choice that reveals character under pressure.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d knows` with a hand rougher than Mira had ever seen from him.

Lin held still over the page as if it might move away. "It looks more real like that."

"It's more real like that," Mira said.

That was the turn. She felt it in her chest like a latch catching. Marisol's name sat on one page, Lin's on the next, Gabe's in the donor-risk section below Jo's old detergent columns. Pool Six led to Pelican Courts. The pink-thread patch linked a walker to a stranger's borrowed motion. A leak file could be disputed. This thing would have to be carried out, copied, and believed by hands that already knew what each line cost.

Jo closed the composition books and stacked them under the ledger. "Master book stays here tonight," she said. "Tomorrow we start duplicate notes in case the city gets energetic."

"I'll take first copy duty," Tavi said.

"You'll do it in longhand like God intended for criminals," Jo said.

"Cruel woman."

"Effective woman."

Mira shut the ledger and felt its weight settle into both palms. Its cloth cover was damp from room steam, its corners softened by old detergent years, and there was fresh ink inside, not yet dry in places. It was ugly, ordinary, and suddenly the most important thing in Blue Hour that was not a breathing person.

The back buzzer sounded.

All five of them turned toward the door, then toward the shelf where the scanner sat dark, then back to the ledger in Mira's hands.

When the buzzer sounded again, she set the book beside the wetbench where the monitor used to be, opened it to a fresh page, and reached for it before she reached for any tool.
state_deltas_required: Yaret's raid hits before the clinic can relocate its clients, files, or conscious overlaps, forcing impossible triage under legal violence.
Blue Hour is destroyed, Gabe is badly re-injured, and the group loses both infrastructure and the illusion that secrecy can still protect them.
